After action(['UP','UP','LEFT']) there are three entries -- first UP, second UP, then LEFT. All change within one entry's frames is that single action animating, never several actions. Step through entries to read per-move motion and causality.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.

You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content: Looking at the image, pressing SPACE seems to have changed the player's appearance. The player's head (white square) is now above the green body. Let me check the segmentation to see what changed.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
seg = current_frame.segmentation
nodes = seg['nodes']
print("Player position:")
for n in nodes:
    if n['color'] in ['W', 'N']:
        print(f"  id={n['id']}, color={n['color']}, boundary={n['boundary']}")

print("\nFrames:")
for n in nodes:
    if n['color'] in ['c', 'G']:
        print(f"  id={n['id']}, color={n['color']}, boundary={n['boundary']}")

# Compare with previous frame
if previous_frame:
    prev_seg = previous_frame.segmentation
    prev_nodes = prev_seg['nodes']
    print("\nPrevious player position:")
    for n in prev_nodes:
        if n['color'] in ['W', 'N']:
            print(f"  id={n['id']}, color={n['color']}, boundary={n['boundary']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting! The player's white head (id=1) has moved from row 28 to row 24. The boundary is [[24, 44], [24, 47], [28, 47], [28, 44]], which means it spans from row 24 to row 28. But wait, the green body (id=7) is still at row 29-31.
So the player's head has moved UP to row 24, which is the same row as the right frame (row 24-27). The right frame is no longer in the segmentation! Let me check if the right frame was absorbed or changed.
Looking at the segmentation, the right frame (which was at row 24-27, col 44-47) is no longer present. The player's white head is now at row 24-28, col 44-47, which overlaps with where the right frame was.
So pressing SPACE caused the player to "absorb" or "collect" the right frame! The right frame is gone, and the player's head has expanded to cover the frame's area.
This is a key discovery! The goal is likely to collect all three frames by:
1. Moving adjacent to the frame (activating it, turning it 'G')
2. Pressing SPACE to collect it
Let me verify this by checking if the right frame is truly gone. The white head now spans from row 24 to row 28, which is the frame's row range plus the player's original head position.
Now I need to collect the other two frames:
- Left frame: row 28-31, col 16-19
- Center-below frame: row 36-39, col 32-35
Let me move to the left frame and collect it.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: Pressing SPACE collected the right frame! The player's white head expanded to cover the frame's area (row 24-28, col 44-47). The right frame is gone.
Goal model: Collect all three frames by moving adjacent and pressing SPACE.
Plan: Move to the left frame (row 28-31, col 16-19), activate it, and press SPACE to collect it.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Move LEFT to the left frame
# Player is at col 44-47, left frame is at col 16-19
# Need to move LEFT by about 7 moves (28 columns)
action(['LEFT', 'LEFT', 'LEFT', 'LEFT', 'LEFT', 'LEFT', 'LEFT'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
